chunk_summary: Children with cystic fibrosis require dietary assessment focusing on pancreatic enzyme adherence, caloric intake, and symptom tracking to address malabsorption and increased energy needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) in managing malabsorption in cystic fibrosis.
Learn methods for assessing dietary intake and caloric needs in children with cystic fibrosis.
Identify barriers to enzyme adherence and strategies for improving nutritional outcomes.
Recognize complications such as CFRD and their impact on dietary management.
key_facts: Children with cystic fibrosis have increased energy expenditure and malabsorption, making normal intake insufficient.
PERT must be taken with every fat-containing meal or snack to prevent greasy, bulky stools and abdominal pain.
Use a 3–7 day food diary to assess variability in intake and link symptoms to specific meals.
High-calorie meals with added oils, dairy, and nut pastes are recommended, paired with correct PERT timing.
In India, delayed diagnosis and inconsistent enzyme access are major barriers; refrigeration constraints should be documented.
Weight velocity and symptom changes should be tracked to evaluate dietary interventions and enzyme optimization.
reference_quote: Children with CF require an integrated dietary and medical review because malabsorption and increased energy expenditure make 'normal' intake insufficient. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Foundation)
document_key_topics: Pancreatic enzyme adherence in cystic fibrosis
Assessment of feeding risks and malabsorption symptoms
Barriers to enzyme access and dietary management in resource-limited settings
